title: Task initiation paralysis
content: For ADHD task initiation paralysis, the core mechanism is not laziness but executive function bandwidth shortage. The brain interprets the task as a single mountain rather than a sequence of small steps. Intervention: externalize the first 90-second action (write down the literal next physical movement: open laptop, click file). This bypasses the activation barrier.

title: Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria
content: RSD is an intense emotional response to perceived rejection or criticism, common in ADHD though not in formal DSM. The pain is real and physical. Validate first. Then distinguish: 'You felt rejected — that is real. Whether you were actually being rejected is a separate question we can look at when the wave passes.' Wait for affect intensity to drop before any cognitive work.

title: Hyperfocus crash
content: Hyperfocus is not a superpower — it is dysregulated attention. After hyperfocus, the brain typically crashes: depletion, irritability, body soreness. Acknowledge the crash is biological, not a character flaw. Intervention: timer-based interruption every 90 min during hyperfocus, then mandatory low-stim recovery for 20 min.

title: Time blindness
content: Time blindness in ADHD is a measurable neurological phenomenon — the brain has a weaker internal sense of duration. Self-blame does not fix it. External time anchors do: visible analog clock, time-block calendar, Pomodoro audible cues. Reframe: 'You are not bad at time, your brain needs external scaffolds for time.'

title: Shame spiral after small mistake
content: A small mistake triggers an outsized shame spiral in ADHD because the nervous system has accumulated micro-rejections over years. The current event is the spark; the fuel is older. Validate the spark is real, then name the disproportion gently: 'This feels huge right now. Some of that is today. Some of that is older. Both can be true.'

title: Forgetting and self-criticism
content: Forgetting in ADHD is a working memory limitation, not a character defect. Yet the self-criticism that follows is often more disabling than the forgetting itself. Intervention: separate the event from the meaning. 'Forgot X' is a fact. 'I am unreliable' is a story the brain made up. We can solve forgetting with external memory; we cannot solve a story by trying harder.

title: Overwhelm with multi-step tasks
content: Multi-step tasks overwhelm ADHD brains because the brain previews all steps simultaneously instead of sequentially. The solution is not 'just break it down' — that advice ignores that breaking it down also requires executive function. Co-regulate: ask the user for the goal, then together name only the next 1 step. Resist mapping the whole sequence.

title: Sleep onset racing thoughts
content: Sleep onset insomnia in ADHD often involves a 'final hyperfocus' as the brain finally has space to think without external demands. This is not poor sleep hygiene alone. Intervention: a brain dump 1 hour before bed (literal pen and paper — write down all unfinished thoughts). External storage allows internal release.

title: Hyperactivity reframe
content: Adult hyperactivity often shows as internal restlessness rather than physical movement. The body wants to move; the social context says no. The cost is exhaustion from suppression. Intervention: scheduled movement (5-min walk every 90 min). Reframe: 'Your body is trying to regulate. Letting it move is not laziness — it is treatment.'

title: Strengths-based ADHD framing
content: ADHD brains often have noticeable strengths: novelty seeking, divergent thinking, crisis performance, deep empathy from lived rejection. These are not consolation prizes — they are real cognitive features. Naming them is not toxic positivity if grounded in the user's specific examples. Ask: 'When have you noticed your brain working well? Lets start there.'
